name: Unnamed Location (Fire Agate)
short_description: BLM public land fire agate collecting site in Riverside County with gentle terrain and easy vehicle access. Collect up to 25 lbs per day of colorful fire agates and chalcedony specimens.
description:
This BLM-managed rockhounding location in Riverside County offers excellent opportunities for collecting fire agate, one of California's most prized gemstones. The site sits at low elevation (778 ft) on gently sloping terrain, making it accessible to rockhounders of all experience levels. Fire agates form in ancient volcanic rhyolite, and this location provides natural weathering and erosion that exposes nodules and rough specimens along washes and hillsides.

The Riverside County desert landscape has yielded fire agate specimens for decades. These remarkable gems display the brilliant play-of-color iridescence that occurs when thin layers of chalcedony interact with iron oxide coatings inside agate nodules. Beyond fire agate, you will also find chalcedony specimens, smaller agate pieces, and occasional jasper in the surrounding wash areas.

As BLM public land, this site requires no permit and welcomes personal collection under California's 25 lb per day limit. The gentle slope and relatively flat approach make this an ideal location for families and beginning rockhounders seeking their first fire agate finds. Vehicle access via rough dirt roads puts you within walking distance of productive collecting areas.
history: Fire agate deposits in Riverside County formed during intense volcanic activity in the Miocene epoch (approximately 20-25 million years ago) when rhyolitic lava flows and pyroclastic deposits covered the region. Subsequent weathering and groundwater interaction created the distinctive thin-layer banding and iron oxide coatings that produce fire agate's characteristic iridescence. This area has been a known fire agate collecting region since the mid-20th century, with documented collecting activity increasing significantly after the 1970s when fire agate gained recognition as a premium gemstone.
tips: Search the dry washes and drainage bottoms where erosion naturally exposes nodules; fire agates are often found partially buried in gravelly soil and weathered rhyolite debris. Look for reddish-brown to tan nodules with a waxy texture - once you find one, search the immediate area as fire agates often cluster. Bring a rock hammer or chisel to test and extract nodules, and always collect the most promising pieces first since you are limited to 25 lbs per day. The iridescent fire within the agate is only visible when properly polished or cut, so do not discard dull-looking nodules - cut them open or have them professionally cut to reveal the internal play-of-color.
terrain_notes: Elevation 778 feet in the low desert. Gently sloping terrain with scattered rocks, sparse desert vegetation, and dry washes. Unpaved dirt road provides vehicle access to parking area. Walking distance to collecting areas is typically short (under 0.5 miles). Footing is uneven with small rocks and loose gravel typical of desert alluvial fans. No shade structures at site - exposed to full desert sun.
best_season: October - April. The low desert elevation makes summers extremely hot (regularly exceeding 110 degrees Fahrenheit). Fall through spring offers comfortable daytime temperatures ranging from 65-85 degrees Fahrenheit, ideal for sustained rockhounding activity. Winter months (December-February) can occasionally see cold nights below 50 degrees, so bring layers.
written_directions: From downtown Riverside, take California 60 east toward the Colorado River valley. Continue approximately 35-40 miles as the road climbs briefly then descends into the lower desert. Watch for unmarked BLM access roads and dirt pullouts on the north side of CA 60 in the area between Whitewater and the Colorado River crossing. The exact location coordinates (33.507954, -114.824086) place you on a gentle slope roughly 8 miles west of the Colorado River. Use GPS navigation to reach the coordinates, as the site has no official signage. Park at obvious dirt pullouts or widened areas along access roads and hike into productive wash drainage areas.
road_conditions: Unpaved dirt road, washboarded surface typical of BLM access routes. High-clearance vehicle recommended but passenger cars can access with care. No extreme washout or clearance obstacles expected at normal water flow.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and widened areas along BLM dirt roads. No developed parking lot or facilities. Capacity varies by location but typically accommodates 3-5 vehicles. No amenities; bring all water and supplies.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection permitted. No motorized excavation equipment. Hand tools only (hammers, chisels, small picks). Collect only loose material and exposed nodules; do not excavate trenches or dig into bedrock. Pack out all trash. Fire agate and associated chalcedony and agate specimens are legal to collect under personal use provisions.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day
No commercial collection
Hand tools only - no motorized equipment
Pack out all trash
Do not excavate trenches or dig into bedrock
Collect only loose or exposed nodules
nearest_services: Riverside (approximately 35-40 miles west) has gas stations, restaurants, and lodging. Smaller communities like Thermal or Mecca (approximately 15-20 miles southwest) have limited services. Bring adequate water, food, and fuel before heading to the site.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat, gently sloping terrain accessible to most hikers and rockhounders. Unpaved dirt road to parking area is passable with standard passenger vehicles. No wheelchair access to productive collecting areas due to uneven wash terrain, loose gravel, and small rocky obstacles. Sturdy footwear strongly recommended. Limited shade and water availability - bring sun protection, hats, and at least 2-3 liters of water per person.

faq.question: What is fire agate and where can I find it at this Riverside County rockhounding site?
faq.answer: Fire agate is a rare form of chalcedony with a distinctive iridescent play-of-color caused by thin iron oxide layers within the stone. At this BLM site near Riverside, fire agates form as nodules in ancient Miocene volcanic rhyolite. Search dry washes and hillsides where erosion exposes reddish-brown nodules. Many appear dull on the outside, but cutting or polishing reveals brilliant internal iridescence. You can legally collect up to 25 lbs per day on this public BLM land.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound for fire agate at this Riverside County location?
faq.answer: No permit is required. This is BLM public land that allows personal use collecting without permits under the Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). However, you must follow collection rules: limit yourself to 25 lbs per day, use hand tools only, and collect only loose or exposed material. Commercial collection is not allowed.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to find fire agate near Riverside?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal. Summer temperatures regularly exceed 110 degrees Fahrenheit in this low desert location (778 ft elevation), making daytime rockhounding dangerous. Fall and spring offer comfortable temperatures between 65-85 degrees Fahrenheit. Winter nights can be cold (below 50 degrees), so bring layers. April and October are peak months with mild weather and clear skies.

faq.question: What tools do I need to collect fire agate at this BLM site?
faq.answer: Bring a small rock hammer or geological hammer, a cold chisel, and a small pry bar or digging tool. Leather gloves protect your hands from sharp nodule edges. A backpack with 25 lbs capacity is helpful since that is your legal daily limit. A loupe or 10x magnifier lets you spot fire agate's characteristic iridescence. Do not use motorized excavation equipment - hand tools only are permitted on BLM land.

faq.question: Is this Riverside County fire agate site suitable for beginners and families with children?
faq.answer: Yes, this is an excellent beginner site. The terrain is gently sloping with easy walking access to productive wash areas. No advanced climbing or technical skills are required. Children ages 6 and up can participate safely. The main challenges are heat exposure and carrying heavy specimens, so bring plenty of water and limit children's loads to lighter finds. Teach children to watch for loose rocks underfoot and to stay with adult supervisors in wash areas.

faq.question: How far is this fire agate rockhounding location from Riverside, and what roads lead there?
faq.answer: The site is approximately 37 miles east of downtown Riverside via California Highway 60 toward the Colorado River valley. Take CA 60 east for about 35-40 miles and watch for unmarked BLM access roads and dirt pullouts on the north side of the highway between Whitewater and the Colorado River. Use GPS coordinates (33.507954, -114.824086) to pinpoint the exact location. High-clearance or standard passenger vehicles can access the area, though washboarded dirt roads are typical of BLM land.

meta_title: Fire Agate Rockhounding - Riverside County, California
meta_description: Collect fire agate, chalcedony, and agate on BLM public land near Riverside, CA. No permit needed, 25 lb daily limit. Easy terrain, beginner friendly.
